items.q: What distinguishes structural isomers from stereoisomers?
items.correct: Structural isomers have different atom connectivity; stereoisomers have the same connectivity but different spatial arrangement.
items.misconception: Stereoisomers are just structural isomers drawn from a different angle.
items.random_wrong: Stereoisomers have different numbers of atoms but the same molecular formula.
items.why: Students don't always grasp that 3D arrangement is chemically meaningful; rotating a drawing on paper feels like the same molecule, so stereoisomerism feels artificial.

items.q: What is the purpose of a salt bridge in an electrochemical cell?
items.correct: It completes the circuit by allowing ions to flow between the half-cells, maintaining electrical neutrality.
items.misconception: The salt bridge carries electrons between the two electrodes.
items.random_wrong: The salt bridge prevents the solutions from reacting with each other.
items.why: Students confuse the internal circuit (ion flow through salt bridge) with the external circuit (electron flow through wire) — both are needed, but they carry different charge carriers.

items.q: How does the rate of an SN2 reaction depend on concentrations?
items.correct: Rate is first order in both substrate AND nucleophile (overall second order) because both are involved in the rate-determining step.
items.misconception: SN2 reactions are first order overall because only the substrate matters.
items.random_wrong: SN2 reactions are zero order because they happen in one step.
items.why: Students confuse SN1 (rate depends only on substrate) and SN2 (rate depends on both); the '2' in SN2 refers to the order, but they forget which way round.
The answer examiners want is: Bloch and Purcell 1946. “Watson and Crick 1953” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Typical O-H stretch in IR appears at:
items.why: Correct answer: 3200-3600 cm-1. “1700 cm-1” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year Bloch and Purcell first demonstrated NMR?
items.why: The real figure is 1946 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
